learnings.title: Asking the agent to mine past conversations for repeated workflows generates useful skills
learnings.markdown:
The conversation log is a mining surface, not just a record: a recurring prompt is an unwritten skill.

One pass over a fortnight of history surfaced thirteen "adopt PR NNNN" conversations re-explaining the same steps — distilled the same day into the `adopting-prs` skill in the skills store — plus a backlog of proposed skills from other repeated prompts (the pasted autoresearch protocol, error-noise triage, bundle-size work, fleet metrics questions).

The skill that comes out of the mining is the evidence the mining works. The interesting follow-on question is cadence: how often is a sweep worth it, and does the yield drop once the obvious repetitions are encoded?

observations.note: two-week history sweep produced adopting-prs (shipped to the skills store) and four more proposed skills

learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: The autoresearch loop protocol is pasted verbatim into every run
learnings.markdown:
Six-plus conversations started with the same long "You are now in autoresearch mode" prompt defining the one-change → measure → report iteration protocol.

The protocol belongs in a skill that takes only the **brief** and the **metric**, so improvements to the protocol propagate to every loop instead of forking per paste. Recent loops targeted backend test wall-time, dashboard endpoint latency, and slow RTL tests.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: The taxonomic filter's async-search races keep producing the same bug
learnings.markdown:
Three separate fixes in two weeks addressed false **"No results"** states in the taxonomic filter:

- stale responses racing fresh queries ([#67499](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/67499) was a partial fix)
- tab switches reusing a stale empty cache instead of refetching
- react-window update loops from churning dependencies (React error #185)

They are one class of bug: search state that doesn't tie results to the query that produced them. The four invariants are now written into the `modifying-taxonomic-filter` skill (a response only applies if it matches the current query; "No results" only renders for a completed search; tab switches refetch; virtualized-list deps stay referentially stable).
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: Moving conventions from instructions to linters pays off immediately
learnings.markdown:
The em-dash rule lived in `AGENTS.md` and was routinely violated; turning it into an automatic commit-time replacement ([#69970](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/69970)) removed the review churn entirely — and the instruction text could then be deleted.

Same shape as enforcing the `StreamingHttpResponse` wrapper with a linter instead of asking people to remember. When a convention is checkable, spend the effort on the linter, not on better prose. The ladder: **linter > lint-staged > skill > instructions**.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: The agent fleet itself needs observability
learnings.markdown:
Recurring questions with no ready-made answer:

- what share of PRs does stamphog approve, and how accurate are its denials?
- what does a PR cost per model?
- are shepherd runs looping on the same PR? (one PR accumulated ~8 "get mergeable" runs)

Several conversations re-derived the same queries from scratch. A skill for assessing the fleet with AI observability and engineering analytics would make these one-liners.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: Bundle-size work has dedicated tooling that isn't documented as a skill
learnings.markdown:
Recurring bundle-size knowledge that lives only in past conversations:

- how to read the **eager graph checker** report on PRs, and what a regression looks like ([#66359](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/66359) grew the authenticated shell by ~80 KiB via static imports)
- which lazy-load patterns safely cut scene edges from the bundle graph
- the toolbar bundle must stay under **10 MB** — CloudFront won't gzip larger files
- the vendored paths-filter ([#67952](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/67952)) exists because dorny/paths-filter can't express include-except-exclude
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: CI/test performance is a continuous campaign with compounding wins
learnings.markdown:
Wins that landed in one two-week stretch, each from measuring first:

- DB-free serializer validation tests replacing full-database tests
- pytest boot-cost cuts ([#69941](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/69941): langsmith and faker disabled, earlier GC window)
- selective flush for TransactionTestCase teardowns ([#69487](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/69487))
- taming jest related-tests fan-out by splitting productScenes ([#69964](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/69964))
- trunk.io flaky detection with quarantine via `.test_quarantine.json`

The measurement queries (slowest tests, hour-by-hour CI speed) are worth encoding alongside `fixing-flaky-tests`.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: CI bots should share one updatable PR comment
learnings.markdown:
Multiple bots each posting their own comment (bundle size, eager graph, hobby deploy, stamphog refusals) buried signal — one PR accumulated pages of stamphog refusal comments.

Consolidating into a **single shared report comment** with a stable section order made PRs readable again. New CI reporters should join that comment rather than posting their own; the hobby deploy smoke test was migrated to it as the pattern.
learnings.author: paul

learnings.title: Every new group type makes the taxonomic filter more complex, and we rarely go the other way
learnings.markdown:
Adding a group type is our standard move for surfacing a new data domain to users — autocapture got one, MCP analytics got one the same way. It works: users can immediately filter, funnel, and path over the new events.

But each group type spends from a complexity budget the taxonomic filter pays: another tab, more ordering and promotion interactions, more empty-search surface (users type the same canonical terms into every tab), and every behavior must be mirrored across the three live variants. Symptoms already show — the single-group-type display was confusing enough to need a fix, and cross-tab "No results" states come partly from tab proliferation.

The worry this strengthens: we make the filter more complex more often than we make it less complex. Treat each new group type as a deliberate spend, and pair additions with consolidation where possible.
learnings.author: paul

observations.note: named unprompted while reviewing the knowledge graph: the complexity worry has predictive weight
